hero.title: Forecast for [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] and historical simulation
subtitleParagraphs: SolSignal 1D is a system that evaluates, on each [[landing-time-horizon|trading day]], where [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] is more likely to move during that day ([[landing-day-up|Up]] / [[landing-day-down|Down]] / [[landing-day-flat|Sideways]]) and converts that forecast into a clear trading scenario: when to enter a trade, what the [[cap-fraction|adaptive position size]] should be, and what the adaptive [[tp-sl|stop-loss]] and [[tp-sl|take-profit]] levels should be.

Alongside the current forecast, the website preserves the history of the same type of decisions, recalculated by past dates on the same strategy from 2021 onward: where the strategy earned, where it was wrong, what [[drawdown|drawdown]] it went through, and how quickly it recovered after weak periods.
bullets: Decision for the nearest trading day: [[signal-direction|position direction]], [[landing-model-confidence|confidence]], and [[risk-layers|risk limits]].
[[landing-prediction-history|An archive of past forecasts]] where each date can be compared with the realized outcome of the same window.
Historical simulation with return, [[drawdown|drawdown]], [[liquidation|liquidations]], and [[recovery|recovery speed after major drawdowns]].

audience.title: Which questions the service answers
audience.subtitle: This layer is used to evaluate the strategy as an operating instrument rather than as one lucky result.
audience.businessLead: SolSignal 1D is used to check whether the same trading strategies produce a repeatable result across long history, what level of risk comes with that result, and whether there is reason to expect similar behavior in future market conditions.
businessQuestions: Is there a real model effect.
What level of risk stands behind the return.
Can the result be checked against facts rather than promises.

workflow.title: How one forecast moves through the full cycle
workflow.subtitle: The project follows the same cycle for every trading day. Model details, thresholds, and risk rules are explained through tooltips and explanatory pages.

forecast.title: Forecast for the trading day
body: At the start of the trading window the system calculates a [[landing-forecast|forecast]] for [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]].
The output contains [[signal-direction|position direction]], [[landing-model-confidence|confidence]], and the final trade scenario after [[risk-layers|risk adjustment]] for that day.

archive.title: Archived before outcome review
body: After publication the calculation enters the [[landing-prediction-history|forecast archive]].
Each date stores both the forecast itself and the realized outcome of that same window after it closes.

validate.title: Simulation across long history
body: The same strategy also goes through [[landing-backtest-summary|historical simulation]] across a long series of trading days.
This layer shows not only return, but also the cost of that result through risk and weak periods.

proof.title: Which evidence supports the result
proof.subtitle: Trust here rests on three sources: a preserved archive, historical simulation, and explicit [[landing-truthfulness|data truthfulness]] rules.

archive.title: Forecast archive
archive.description: The archive confirms that past forecasts are preserved by date and can be compared with the realized outcome of the same window.
archive.errorDescription: The archive index could not be loaded for the history card.

backtest.title: Historical simulation
backtest.description: The summary shows how this strategy would behave on long history: across return, risk, and emergency events.
backtest.errorDescription: The key metrics could not be extracted from the summary report.

truth.title: Data truthfulness
truth.description: The truthfulness section describes which data is available at calculation time and how the project separates the forecast from future outcome.

realJournal.title: [[landing-real-forecast-journal|Real forecast journal]]
realJournal.subtitle: This section accumulates new trading days after conditional live trading started on March 12, 2026. Unlike the [[landing-prediction-history|archive of past forecasts]], it grows only as new dates appear.
realJournal.description: This journal is needed to compare [[landing-backtest-summary|historical simulation]] and [[landing-model-metrics|model metrics]] with how the strategy behaves after the project has moved into a conditional live mode.
bullets: Unlike the [[landing-prediction-history|archive of past forecasts]], which is recalculated from historical dates, this journal grows only through new days starting from March 12, 2026.
For each of these days the forecast is fixed in advance, while the outcome is still unknown, and the comparison with reality appears only after that same day window closes.
Even a long [[landing-backtest-summary|historical simulation]] does not prove by itself that there is no [[landing-leakage-guards|leakage]] in the data or the calculation path.

The journal makes it possible to see whether the realized result on these new days stays aligned with what [[landing-model-metrics|model metrics]] and [[landing-backtest-summary|historical simulation]] had suggested earlier.
realJournal.errorDescription: The summary of real trading days could not be loaded for the dedicated block on the main page.

facts.pendingAccuracy: Will appear after the first finalized day

megaTable.title: [[landing-policy-branch-mega|Policy Branch Mega]]
megaTable.subtitle: This section is used for detailed strategy comparison. The final reading of a [[policy|Policy]] usually comes down to this table.
megaTable.description: [[landing-policy-branch-mega|Policy Branch Mega]] gathers every [[policy|Policy]] configuration on one long history: [[branch|BASE / ANTI-D branches]], [[sl-mode-term|SL modes]], [[filters|filters]], and the final risk/return metrics. That makes it possible to read not only final return, but also the price of that result through drawdown, liquidations, recovery, and trade structure.
bullets: The table shows [[total-pnl|return]], [[drawdown|drawdown]], [[liquidation|liquidations]], [[recovery|recovery]], trade share, and days without entry into a trade side by side.
The comparison is valid because all configurations are calculated on the same history and on the same trading days.
The mini demo on the main page shows only one top [[policy|Policy]]. A full reading requires the full [[landing-policy-branch-mega|Policy Branch Mega]] table.

sandbox.title: Why the project matters beyond current return
sandbox.subtitle: Even if the current return of the top strategy is not yet the main reason to buy the project on its own, the product can already be used as a sandbox for testing new trading hypotheses and practical theories.
sandbox.description: The project can already be used as an operating environment for testing ideas. It already contains the [[landing-forecast|forecast]] layer, [[landing-backtest-summary|historical simulation]], [[policy|Policy]] comparison, the [[landing-real-forecast-journal|real forecast journal]], and [[landing-diagnostics|diagnostics]].
bullets: The [[landing-forecast|current forecast]] shows how a new idea looks on the nearest trading day and what trade scenario it becomes.

[[landing-backtest-summary|Historical simulation]] and the [[landing-policy-branch-mega|main strategy table]] make it possible to compare hypotheses across return, [[drawdown|drawdown]], [[liquidation|liquidations]], trade share, and days without entering a trade.
The [[landing-real-forecast-journal|real forecast journal]] adds a separate validation layer on new dates after the conditional live mode started.

[[landing-diagnostics|Diagnostics]], the [[landing-explain|explain layer]], and [[landing-model-metrics|model metrics]] show why a result appeared, where strategy quality weakens, and which [[filters|filters]] materially change the outcome.

tooltipRules.solana:
Solana is the base asset of the [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] pair. In this product the focus is not the network itself but the tradable instrument used for forecast, history, and historical simulation.

What it shows:
when the interface mentions Solana, it refers to the SOL price inside the working trading pair and to how the strategy behaves on that asset.

How to read it:
the term is here to separate the traded asset from the model or strategy. The website evaluates trading logic on SOL price behavior, not the broader “Solana project” narrative.
tooltipRules.solUsdt:
[[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] is a Binance trading pair: how many units of USDT the market currently pays for 1 SOL. This is the exact instrument used for forecast, forecast history, and historical simulation on the public website.

What it shows:
it is the main trading object of the product. Current forecast, historical archive, and backtest are intentionally kept on the same pair so the user compares one instrument instead of a mixed basket of coins.

How to read it:
if a result looks strong, that means it is strong on SOL/USDT specifically. Porting the conclusion to another asset requires separate validation because volatility, liquidity, and execution statistics will differ.
tooltipRules.mlProject:
An ML project is a product where the trading decision is built by a machine-learning model rather than by a manually written analyst rule. Here the model turns historical data into features and produces a forecast for one trading day.

What it shows:
the term refers to the whole operating loop, not just to one model file: live forecast, archive of past decisions, long-history simulation, and risk diagnostics all belong to the same decision system.

How to read it:
the project should be judged through repeatability, not through one nice live card. The real question is whether the same logic survives history, keeps risk under control, and transfers to new days.
tooltipRules.mlModel:
An ML model is the algorithm that estimates the scenario of one trading day for [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] from historical market data. Its probabilities are later passed through policy rules and risk layers.

What it shows:
the model owns the forecasting part of the pipeline: it transforms features into a directional expectation before trading policy decides whether that expectation is tradable.

How to read it:
the model cannot be judged by one probability snapshot alone. Its value is determined by whether the same logic transfers to unseen days and behaves coherently in long-history simulation.
tooltipRules.modelWeights:
Model weights are not a memory of exact days. They are the internal numbers that remain inside the model after training.

What it shows:
1) During training history is not stored as a list of ready-made answers for each day.
2) History changes the model through these numbers: good and bad outcomes gradually push the weights in different directions.
3) If an old forecast changes after a recalculation, that means the trained version of the model changed and its weights changed with it.

How to read it:
1) Completed days first help adjust the weights.
2) During inference the engine then uses those ready weights and sees only the data of the current day.
3) The realized day outcome is attached to the report later as a separate step.
tooltipRules.btc:
BTC is used as an external market reference alongside [[landing-solana|Solana]]. It helps the model see whether [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] is moving because of the broader crypto regime or because of a more local asset-specific story.

What it shows:
this does not mean the product trades Bitcoin directly. BTC acts as a context signal that helps separate market-wide movement from local behavior in the target pair.

How to read it:
if BTC becomes influential in the forecast, it means the model is leaning more on broad crypto conditions rather than on SOL-only behavior. That changes how the forecast should be interpreted.
tooltipRules.macroIndicators:
Macro indicators are external market signals that describe the broader regime around the core trading instrument. They help the model separate local [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] movement from larger market conditions.

What it shows:
these indicators do not open a trade by themselves. They provide context such as calm market, stress regime, elevated volatility, or synchronized sector movement.

How to read it:
if macro signals become more influential, the forecast should be read as more dependent on the overall market regime rather than on the isolated behavior of SOL/USDT.
tooltipRules.features:
Features are the numeric inputs of the model. They are built from price, volatility, [[landing-btc|BTC]] behavior, and other observable [[factor|factors]] before being transformed into a day-level forecast.

What it shows:
a feature is not a ready trading decision. It is raw input that becomes useful only after the model compares it with history and with the rest of the feature set.

How to read it:
when explain or PFI highlights an important feature, the right reading is not “this single number prints money”. It means the model loses meaningful context when that signal becomes unavailable.
tooltipRules.signal:
A signal is the model output for one [[landing-time-horizon|trading day]]: position direction, [[landing-model-confidence|confidence]], and risk constraints. That output then goes through the [[landing-trading-policy|trading policy]].

What it shows:
a signal is not automatically the same thing as an executed trade. Policy rules, risk sizing, filters, and admission checks still decide whether the market entry is actually allowed.

How to read it:
if a signal looks strong but no trade appears, the problem is no longer the model direction itself. The next place to inspect is policy or risk logic, not the forecast class alone.
tooltipRules.forecast:
A forecast is the expected scenario for [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] over one working trading window. The same forecast format is used both for the nearest day and for historical dates in the archive.

What it shows:
this is the core object the website revolves around: the system forms an expectation in advance and then lets the user compare that expectation with the realized outcome of the same window.

How to read it:
a forecast becomes useful only when paired with historical validation. One attractive answer for today means little if the same decision logic does not hold on archived days and historical simulation.
tooltipRules.currentPrediction:
Current prediction is the page for the nearest decision on [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] for one [[landing-time-horizon|trading day]]. It shows the main calculation for the current window and, when available ahead of time, the early preview for that same day.

What it shows:
the page answers the practical question “what does the system expect now” and with which risk limits that expectation is presented before or near the start of the working session.

How to read it:
it should be read together with prediction history and historical simulation. By itself, the current card is only one day; strategy quality is judged by how the same type of decision behaves across many dates.
tooltipRules.predictionHistory:
Prediction history is the archive of past forecasts by date. Each date stores both the original forecast and the realized outcome of the same trading window after it closed.

What it shows:
this is the closest view to a live stream of decisions: what the system expected in advance and what actually happened later in that exact day window.

How to read it:
history matters because it lets the user test honesty and repeatability. It shows where forecasts hold, where they fail, and which market regimes produce the most errors.
tooltipRules.backtestSummary:
Backtest summary is the long-history simulation page for the strategy. It shows not only return but also the price of that result through [[drawdown|drawdown]], [[liquidation|liquidations]], and recovery behavior.

What it shows:
this layer exists to compare strategies on the same historical days and see what paid for the profit: rare trading, deep drawdowns, emergency events, or a calmer risk profile.

How to read it:
if a strategy looks good only by total return, the summary exposes that quickly. This is where the user sees whether the result is robust or whether it collapses in bad periods.
tooltipRules.diagnostics:
Diagnostics is the set of pages that shows where the strategy loses quality: at entry, in the risk filter, in no-trade days, in specific market regimes, or later during execution.

What it shows:
this is not another showcase of nice numbers. It is the cause-finding layer that shows why trades are skipped, where emergency risk accumulates, and which part of the pipeline really damages the result.

How to read it:
diagnostics matters when one summary table is no longer enough. If a strategy underperforms, diagnostics separates model weakness from policy weakness and from execution weakness.
tooltipRules.analysis:
Analysis is the comparison layer for strategies, confidence slices, and execution modes on the same underlying data. It is used when one headline number is no longer enough and several alternatives must be read side by side.

What it shows:
the point is not to pick a winner blindly but to expose the structure of differences: which mode earns more, where risk increases, how no-trade behavior changes, and what happens across buckets or confidence groups.

How to read it:
this section turns comparison into an observable process. It becomes especially useful when several configurations produce similar return but pay for it through very different risk profiles.
tooltipRules.explain:
Explain is the plain-language layer for the project terms, pages, and mechanics. It exists so the user can understand what the metrics mean and how models, risk layers, and historical reports connect to one another.

What it shows:
it does not recompute the market and it does not invent another analytical layer. Its role is to translate internal mechanics into definitions and reading rules that remain stable across the interface.

How to read it:
when a number looks unclear, explain should remove ambiguity: what the metric is, where it is calculated, why it matters, and how it should be compared with neighboring values.
tooltipRules.truthfulness:
Data truthfulness is the section about what the system was allowed to know at forecast time, how the realized day outcome is computed separately, and why future information must not flow back into the decision.

What it shows:
this is where the project draws the boundary between causal decision-making and post-fact evaluation. Without that boundary, good-looking historical numbers stop being evidence of real model quality.

How to read it:
the section should be read as the trust contract of the product. If this contract is broken, attractive metrics in history no longer prove that the strategy would behave the same way in live conditions.
tooltipRules.timeHorizon:
A trading day is the working window of the project for one trading day, not a full calendar day.

What that means:
entry, realized labels, and the [[backtest|backtest]] are tied to one consistent working window that is counted against the [[landing-nyse-session|U.S. regular session]] of that same day, rather than against arbitrary UTC calendar days.

The [[landing-early-preview|early preview]] belongs to that same trading day, but is published earlier, before the main session opens.

Weekends are excluded from this window. [[why-weekends|Why?]]
tooltipRules.timeHorizonWhy:
Why the calculation is anchored to New York:
the start of the main US trading session usually brings more liquidity and stronger intraday movement than the quieter hours before it.

What this choice gives the project:
volatility becomes closer to the conditions where the strategy actually has to decide, and metrics such as the [[atr-indicator|ATR indicator]] describe the active market better than a calm slice of the day. Weekends are excluded for the same reason: activity there is usually even lower, so features and volatility drift further away from the working market regime.

How to read it:
the model is trained and validated on the part of the trading day where movement is more likely to move from noise into a tradable scenario.
tooltipRules.nyseSession:
The U.S. regular session is the regular NYSE session of that same [[landing-time-horizon|trading day]].

What it shows:
the day-window entry is counted from the session open at 09:30 New York time. That is 13:30 UTC during DST and 14:30 UTC in winter.

How to read it:
the base end-of-day boundary is counted from the actual close of that same session. On a normal day the exchange closes at 16:00 New York time, and baseline-exit is set to 15:58 New York time. That is 19:58 UTC during DST and 20:58 UTC in winter.

Example:
if the exchange closes earlier than usual, the end of the window also shifts earlier, because baseline-exit is always calculated from the factual close of that same session.

[[landing-time-horizon-why|Why is the calculation anchored to this session?]]
tooltipRules.dayUp:
Up is the day scenario where the model expects price to move mainly upward inside the working trading window.

What it shows:
the class does not merely mean a green candle. It means the system sees enough probability skew toward upward movement to treat the day as directionally tradable.

How to read it:
the label should still be read together with policy and risk controls. An Up day can still end as no-trade or as a cautious trade if later layers decide the entry is too risky.
tooltipRules.dayDown:
Down is the day scenario where the model expects price to move mainly downward inside the working trading window.

What it shows:
the class represents a directional downside bias inside the selected window rather than a random negative tick.

How to read it:
just like Up, this is a forecast direction rather than a guaranteed executed trade. Policy rules and risk filters still decide whether that view is tradable.
tooltipRules.dayFlat:
Sideways is the day scenario without a strong directional move, where price is more likely to stay inside a range than to break clearly up or down.

What it shows:
the class exists to separate weak, noisy days from days where the market shows a usable directional edge.

How to read it:
a Sideways label does not mean the market was frozen. It means the movement did not look stable or strong enough for a confident base directional call.
tooltipRules.spotPolicy:
A spot strategy is a strategy without borrowed [[leverage|leverage]]. Trades are opened only on the strategy's own capital without margin amplification.

What it shows:
this term matters in the top-strategy block because it separates a result earned on owned capital from a result that would have been amplified by exchange credit.

How to read it:
if the strategy stays spot, return and risk here depend on trade direction, position size, and ordinary exit rules rather than extra margin acceleration.
tooltipRules.realForecastJournal:
The real forecast journal is the section with new dates after the conditional live mode started on March 12, 2026.

What it shows:
unlike the [[landing-prediction-history|archive of past forecasts]], this journal does not recalculate old dates and accumulates only new days as they arrive.

How to read it:
this section is used to compare the realized outcome of new days with what [[landing-backtest-summary|historical simulation]] and [[landing-model-metrics|model metrics]] showed earlier.
tooltipRules.reinvestment:
Reinvestment means putting already earned profit back into the active trading balance so it can participate in future trades.

What changes in the numbers:
if profit is reinvested, it stays inside the working balance and affects the size of future trades. If profit is not reinvested, it is withdrawn separately and no longer participates in new entries.

How to read it:
in the top-strategy block this term separates total earnings, the part already withdrawn, and the money that still remains in the active trading balance.
tooltipRules.pathBasedLabeling:
Path-based labeling is a way to label historical days not only by close price but also by the path price traveled inside the window. It helps separate a tradable move from a formally similar but unstable scenario.

What it shows:
the method looks beyond one final tick and considers how the day behaved inside the window: whether the move was smooth, choppy, quickly reversed, or structurally weak for trading.

How to read it:
it should be read as an attempt to make historical truth closer to real trading. The model sees not only how the day ended, but also whether the way it got there looked tradable.
tooltipRules.earlyPreview:
Early preview is the advance publication of the same [[landing-time-horizon|trading day]] forecast before the main session begins.

What it shows:
it does not describe another day and it does not replace the main forecast. It gives the same day-level decision earlier, when the market context is already fixed but the regular working session has not opened yet.

How to read it:
early preview is useful when the user wants to see whether the system already had a stable directional view before the main window started. The key comparison is not “preview versus live day”, but preview versus the later main forecast of that same trading day.
tooltipRules.microModel:
Micro model is the refinement layer that works only on ambiguous [[landing-day-flat|sideways]] days.

What it shows:
its role is not to rebuild the whole forecast from scratch. It looks for a weak directional tilt exactly where the base daily layer is still too flat to issue a confident growth or decline call.

How to read it:
this model matters only on the subset of uncertain days. If it improves the final decision, that means the system found useful extra structure inside otherwise flat-looking market conditions rather than replacing the base daily model.
tooltipRules.multiLayer:
Multi-layer architecture means the project does not rely on one anonymous score. It combines the daily layer, micro refinement, and SL-aware risk interpretation into one stacked decision pipeline.

What it shows:
each layer owns a different responsibility: base day direction, refinement of weak flat days, and risk-aware correction of execution quality.

How to read it:
if the final result changes, the question is not only whether the model was right or wrong. The useful reading is which layer changed the answer and whether that change improved risk, transfer, or execution quality.
tooltipRules.slRisk:
SL risk is the execution-risk layer that estimates whether the trade is likely to hit [[landing-sl-model|stop-loss]] before it reaches the intended profit path.

What it shows:
it does not choose market direction on its own. It evaluates whether a direction that already looks tradable is still acceptable once exit-risk quality is taken into account.

How to read it:
if the final forecast becomes more cautious than the base directional view, the first place to inspect is the SL-aware layer. That usually means the market still has direction, but the entry became less safe to execute.
tooltipRules.guardrail:
Guardrail is a hard safety rule that blocks or limits actions before a risky state turns into a misleading result.

What it shows:
guardrails are not cosmetic warnings. They are explicit barriers against invalid slices, unstable inputs, broken report states, or risk conditions that should not be silently passed through to the user.

How to read it:
if a guardrail fires, the correct interpretation is not “the system is being picky”. It means the product is refusing to pretend that an unsafe or invalid state is still a trustworthy forecast or backtest output.
tooltipRules.specificity:
Specificity is the share of good base-trade days among all days that the model classified as safe enough to avoid the bad-trade class.

What it shows:
it answers how often the system correctly rejects clearly bad trading days without confusing too many good ones. In diagnostics this matters because the model is judged not only by finding opportunities, but also by avoiding harmful entries.

How to read it:
high specificity means the model is better at protecting the strategy from obviously bad trade days. Low specificity means the “safe” side still contains too many days that later behave like the bad class.
tooltipRules.blameSplit:
Blame split is the attribution layer that assigns a weak result to the dominant failure bucket instead of leaving all bad days in one undifferentiated pile.

What it shows:
it separates whether the problem came from the model itself, from policy restrictions, from execution quality, or from other downstream conditions.

How to read it:
this layer matters because the same weak PnL can come from very different causes. Blame split makes the cause observable, so the next fix targets the real bottleneck instead of the most visible symptom.
tooltipRules.hotspots:
Hotspots are the parts of the pipeline where the strategy most often loses quality, accumulates risk, or produces unstable behavior.

What it shows:
this is not one metric by itself. It is the reading layer that highlights where weak outcomes cluster: model-side, policy-side, execution-side, or data-quality-side.

How to read it:
if the same hotspot keeps dominating different tables, the problem is structural rather than accidental. That is usually the place where the next fix gives the highest impact on truthfulness or strategy quality.
tooltipRules.pfi:
PFI (permutation feature importance) measures how much the model loses quality when one feature is intentionally broken or shuffled while the rest stay intact.

What it shows:
PFI does not say that one feature alone “causes” the forecast. It shows how dependent the model is on that feature for keeping its current quality level.

How to read it:
a high PFI means the model materially degrades without that feature. A near-zero PFI means the feature adds little unique value, even if it still looks intuitive to a human reader.
tooltipRules.confusionMatrix:
Confusion matrix is the table that compares predicted classes with realized classes for the same evaluation window.

What it shows:
it makes class errors visible by direction: which outcomes are recognized correctly, which ones are confused with each other, and where the model most often overstates or understates a scenario.

How to read it:
this table matters because one headline score can hide asymmetric mistakes. A model may look acceptable overall while still repeatedly confusing one specific class that is critical for trading decisions.
tooltipRules.modelMetrics:
Model metrics are the quality measures used to judge whether a forecasting layer is stable, transferable, and worth trusting on new data.

What it shows:
these metrics are not only about accuracy. They describe calibration, class balance, error structure, and the gap between seen history and unseen evaluation slices.

How to read it:
strong model metrics matter only when they remain coherent across Train, OOS, diagnostics, and backtest behavior. A good score in isolation is weaker evidence than a consistent profile across these layers.
tooltipRules.policyBranchMega:
Policy Branch Mega is the full historical table for every [[policy|Policy]] configuration on one long history. It keeps [[branch|BASE / ANTI-D branches]], SL modes, filters, return, drawdown, liquidations, long/short structure, and recovery in one place.

What it shows:
this page is built for comparing not one isolated strategy idea, but the whole layer of Policy configurations on identical trading days.

How to read it:
this mega table should not be read by TotalPnl% alone. Its purpose is to show which configuration stays robust and which one buys the result with deeper drawdowns, liquidations, stricter filters, or weaker recovery.
tooltipRules.noTrade:
No-trade is the state where the system intentionally does not open a position for that day.

What it shows:
it does not mean the pipeline failed. It means some stage decided that the expected edge was too weak or the risk conditions were too poor to justify an entry.

How to read it:
a high no-trade share is not automatically bad. It means the strategy is selective, and that selectivity must be judged together with later risk and quality outcomes rather than treated as missing output.
tooltipRules.attribution:
Attribution is the reading layer that explains which component or reason contributed most to the final result, weak period, or blocked trade.

What it shows:
it turns a blended outcome into an interpretable structure: what came from the model, from policy logic, from execution path, or from a later safety restriction.

How to read it:
attribution matters when one aggregate number is no longer enough. It tells whether the next action should target model quality, risk rules, or trade execution rather than changing everything at once.
tooltipRules.featureImportance:
Feature importance is the layer that ranks which inputs matter most for the model and which ones change the result only marginally.

What it shows:
it does not promise causal truth by itself. It shows where the forecasting pipeline depends most on information content and where removing a feature would change the model least.

How to read it:
feature importance is strongest when it is read together with explain and PFI. If the same feature stays important across those layers, its role in the decision is much more credible than in a single ranking alone.
tooltipRules.predictionAggregation:
Prediction aggregation is the step where outputs of several model layers are combined into one final probability view.

What it shows:
this step collects the daily layer, micro refinement, and risk-aware interpretation into the final scenario ranking that the strategy later consumes.

How to read it:
aggregation should be read as a structured merge, not as a blind average. If the final answer changes, the useful question is which layer shifted the balance and whether that shift improved robustness or only changed the final label.
tooltipRules.allHistory:
Full history is the mode where the model takes the whole completed history up to the latest day whose [[landing-time-horizon|trading day]] outcome is already known, trains on that full range, and then recalculates the whole history with that same trained version.

What it shows:
1) The most fully trained version of the model on the whole available past.
2) History with no split between [[train-segment|Train]] and [[landing-oos|OOS]].
3) A mode where the same day may appear both in training and in the repeated forecast pass.

How to read it:
1) History first changes only the [[landing-model-weights|model weights]].
2) During inference the ready-made day outcome is no longer fed into the model: the engine sees only day data, and the realized outcome is attached to the report later.
3) That is why old full forecasts may change not because the day was memorized directly, but because the refit model changed its weights.
4) For an honest quality check [[landing-all-history|Full]] should always be compared with [[landing-oos|OOS]].
tooltipRules.recentTailHistory:
Recent tail is not a separate training mode. It is only the freshest end of an already built [[landing-oos|OOS]] slice.

What it shows:
1) The system first builds ordinary [[landing-oos|OOS]] and only then keeps up to the latest 240 OOS records.
2) If the whole OOS slice is shorter than that limit, the tail becomes the full OOS slice.
3) This mode isolates what is happening on the most recent days of the honest evaluation.

How to read it:
1) The tail is selected from already built OOS records rather than from a new calendar rule.
2) No new model is trained here and no new prediction pass is launched.
3) If OOS still looks stable while the tail is already weaker, deterioration starts on the most recent days.
tooltipRules.baselineBacktest:
Baseline backtest is the reference historical simulation under the main production-like setup used by the project.

What it shows:
it is the default comparison point for new variants, filters, and strategy changes. The point of the baseline is not to be “best”, but to provide a stable anchor for judging whether later modifications really improve the profile.

How to read it:
when an experimental variant looks better, the right question is not only whether return increased. The comparison must also show what happened to drawdown, liquidation exposure, and recovery difficulty relative to the baseline.
tooltipRules.experimentalBacktest:
Experimental backtest is a non-baseline historical simulation used to test a modified rule set, filter, layer, or execution mode against the reference setup.

What it shows:
it exists to answer whether the new idea changes the result for the better or only moves risk into another place.

How to read it:
an experimental result should never be judged by one attractive metric in isolation. It must beat or clarify the baseline across both return and risk, otherwise it is just a different profile, not a better one.
tooltipRules.modelConfidence:
Model confidence is the strength of the model's belief in the selected scenario for the trading day.

What it shows:
confidence is not the same thing as guaranteed correctness. It is the internal strength of the probability edge that later controls whether the strategy trades, how strict filters become, and which risk layers are allowed to engage.

How to read it:
high confidence matters only when history supports it. A strong-looking confidence value without stable behavior in OOS, diagnostics, and backtests is still weak evidence.
tooltipRules.liquidationBuffer:
Liquidation buffer is the safety distance between the current trade state and the forced-close threshold.

What it shows:
it measures how much room the position still has before liquidation risk turns from theoretical into immediate. The smaller the buffer, the less tolerance remains for adverse price movement.

How to read it:
a shrinking buffer is a warning about execution fragility, not just about return. Even a profitable strategy becomes structurally unsafe if many trades keep operating too close to forced-close territory.
tooltipRules.liqUnreachableBucket:
Liquidation is unreachable is a note shown for a [[policy|Policy]] when the [[liquidation|liquidation]] level cannot be reached.

What it shows:
for this specific [[policy|Policy]] the [[bucket|bucket]] has more capital than the cash size of the [[position|position]], so there is no critical level where the exchange would be forced to liquidate due to insufficient margin.

How to read it:
this does not mean the trade is risk-free. It only means the liquidation scenario does not apply to this specific [[policy|Policy]].

summary.verdictRisk: Emergency risk looks contained — [[drawdown|drawdown]] is {{maxDdPct}}, and there were no forced [[liquidation|liquidations]] on this stretch.
summary.verdictActivity: The strategy does not trade every day — about {{noTradePct}} of days end without entering a trade, and average activity stays near {{tradesPerDay}} trades per trading day.

hero.title: Analysis
hero.subtitle: Analytical reports on PnL, top trades/days, and strategy behavior. This is the quickest way to see where the strategy earns, where it loses, and under which market regimes.

overview.title: How to read the analysis layer
overview.subtitle: The top block exists to route the first question to the right analytical screen: where to find extremes, where to compare Policy variants, where to inspect market regimes, and where to trace one number through the calculation path.

extremes.question: The quickest view of the best and worst days or trades is needed
extremes.answer: The starting point is Policy ratings. It shows extreme trades, extreme days, and the base capital profile of the top configurations.

policyComparison.question: All [[policy|Policy]] configurations must be compared on one long history: branches, filters, and risk profile
policyComparison.answer: The owner page for that question is [[landing-policy-branch-mega|Policy Branch Mega]]. One mega table keeps every Policy configuration together: [[branch|BASE / ANTI-D branches]], SL modes, filters, return, [[drawdown|MaxDD%]], liquidations, recovery, and trade structure.

regimes.question: The market regimes where the strategy is strong or weak must be understood
regimes.answer: The pair Day statistics plus Confidence and TP/SL shows behavior by day type, weekday, buckets of [[landing-model-confidence|model confidence]], and excursion room before trade exit.

actualVsForecast.question: The forecast must be checked against the realized outcome on new days
actualVsForecast.answer: Real Forecast Journal compares forecast and actual outcome on real days that appeared after the semi-live contour started.

formulaTrace.question: A concrete metric must be traced back to its formula path
formulaTrace.answer: Execution pipeline breaks one report row into layers: model, trading decision, execution, money, and final [[landing-aggregation|aggregation]].

rootCause.question: The numbers are already clear, but the reason for the failure is needed
rootCause.answer: That is where analysis stops. The reason behind drawdown, extra [[landing-no-trade|NoTrade]], or an overly strict filter lives in Diagnostics.

steps.ratingsFirst: Start with Policy ratings. That page is the fastest way to see where the result is extremely good or extremely bad.
steps.compareThen: Then move to [[landing-policy-branch-mega|Policy Branch Mega]]: here the comparison is no longer about one episode, but about the full layer of Policy configurations on a long history.
steps.regimesThen: After that the regime layer is chosen: Day statistics answers for day types and weekdays, while Confidence and TP/SL answers for the relation between signal, risk, and price room.
steps.traceFinally: When a single number must be verified all the way down to the formula, the last stop is Execution pipeline.

analysisScope.title: Which three layers are collected here

bullets.outcomeLayer: Outcome layer: ratings and [[landing-policy-branch-mega|Policy Branch Mega]] answer for the overall historical result of the strategy.
bullets.regimeLayer: Regime layer: day stats, confidence-risk, and real forecast journal show where the strategy holds up and where it loses stability.
bullets.pipelineLayer: Formula layer: execution pipeline decomposes the ready result into model, decision, execution, and money.

boundaries.title: Boundary between analysis and neighboring sections

bullets.analysisVsDiagnostics: Analysis answers the question "what happened in the numbers". It shows return, [[drawdown|drawdown]], trade structure, and market regimes.
bullets.analysisVsGuide: Diagnostics answers the question "why it happened". That route starts once the result is already visible but the defect source must be localized.
bullets.analysisVsDeveloper: Guide explains terms and rules. Developer shows the internal system structure and report assembly.

ratings.title: Policy ratings
ratings.description: Best and worst trades/days by outcome, plus summary Equity/DD for top-3 policies. Ideal entry point to find extreme drawdowns and unusually successful periods.

policyBranchMega.title: Policy Branch Mega
policyBranchMega.description: The most complete summary for all Policy configurations on a long history: BASE / ANTI-D branches, SL modes, filters, return, risk, stability, recovery, long/short split, and average growth pace. This is the main table for comparing strategy configurations on one trading-day set.

policySetups.title: Policy setup history
policySetups.description:
This page shows one trading configuration across its full history: [[landing-sol-usdt|SOL/USDT]] candles, day blocks, entry and exit points, [[tp-sl|TP/SL]] levels, and the working balance curve.

It is the right screen when summary percentages from the mega table are no longer enough and the time path itself matters: long weak periods, speed of recovery, and the exact way trades closed inside each day.

dayStats.title: Day statistics
dayStats.description: Breakdowns by day types (UP/DOWN/FLAT) and weekdays: WinRate, NoTrade share, and missed opportunity. Helps identify the regime where the strategy has the strongest edge.

confidenceRisk.title: Confidence and TP/SL
confidenceRisk.description: Bucket statistics by model confidence: TP/SL hit frequency and MFE/MAE dynamics across signal levels. Helps decide where to increase take-profit and where to reduce risk.

realForecastJournal.title: Real Forecast Journal
realForecastJournal.description:
The section compares forecast and realized outcome on real days that appeared after the semi-live contour started.

The second layer shows where the forecast matched the day result, how quality behaves on new dates, and how close that picture stays to historical expectations.

executionPipeline.title: Execution pipeline
executionPipeline.description: End-to-end report by computation layers: model, decision, execution, accounting, and final aggregation. Useful for step-by-step formula validation and mismatch analysis.

sectionDescriptions.executionLevel: Trade execution layer: entry sources, actual exit reasons, and liquidation reach/trigger facts.

sectionDescriptions.aggregationLevel: Final policy-level metric aggregation: TotalPnl, Drawdown, WinRate, liquidations, and withdrawn profit.

hero.title: Diagnostics
hero.subtitle: Map of diagnostic reports: risk, guardrail, decisions, and hotspots. Each block is a dedicated page with detailed tables showing drawdown causes and model errors.

overview.title: How to read the diagnostics layer
overview.subtitle: The top block exists so that failure analysis does not start blindly: first the symptom type is chosen, then the right diagnostic screen is opened, and only after that the detailed tables are read.

factTable.title: Which symptom opens which diagnostic screen

drawdown.question: The reason capital sank or trades approached forced closure must be understood
drawdown.answer: The starting point is Risk and liquidations. It shows [[active-equity|Equity]], [[drawdown|MaxDD%]], [[liquidation|liquidations]], and the remaining distance to forced closure.

filter.question: The question is whether the protective filter saves the result or cuts too many valid entries
filter.answer: The guardrail page shows [[landing-guardrail|guardrail]], [[landing-specificity|specificity]], and the error structure for blocked versus allowed days.

actor.question: The actor that pushed the day into error must be identified
actor.answer: The decisions page decomposes the route through [[landing-attribution|Attribution]] and [[landing-blame-split|blame-split]]: the source can be the signal, the filter, the [[policy|Policy]], or a combination of them.

repetition.question: The place where the problem repeats as a pattern must be understood
repetition.answer: Hotspots gathers recurring regimes with extra [[landing-no-trade|NoTrade]], weak coverage, and conflicting decisions.

leftovers.question: A rare or new table is needed and it still has no dedicated group
leftovers.answer: Other is not the default entry point. It is opened when the symptom no longer fits risk, guardrail, decisions, or hotspots.

finishedResult.question: The cause is already localized, but the full strategy picture must be revisited
finishedResult.answer: The route returns to Analysis for the overall picture of return, regimes, and stability.

steps.riskFirst: The first stop is the risk layer: drawdown, liquidations, and proximity to forced closure show the price of the weak result fastest.
steps.filterSecond: The second step is guardrail: this is where it becomes visible whether the protective filter really removed bad days rather than useful entries together with them.
steps.actorThird: The third step is decisions: that screen answers who actually pushed the day into error and at which stage the route turned wrong.
steps.clusterFourth: The fourth step is hotspots: it is used when the target is not one episode but a repeating weak regime.
steps.leftoversFifth: Other is opened last, when the symptom no longer belongs to any of the main diagnostic groups.

diagnosticsScope.title: Which kinds of causes live here

bullets.riskSurface: Risk layer shows where the result breaks through drawdown, liquidations, and proximity to forced closure.
bullets.filterSurface: Filter layer shows how [[landing-guardrail|guardrail]] changes day admission and whether useful entries are cut together with bad ones.
bullets.actorSurface: Actor layer shows which node really pushed the day into error: the base signal, the filter, the [[policy|Policy]], or a combination of them.
bullets.clusterSurface: Cluster layer shows where the problem is not random but repeats across the same regime or day type.

boundaries.title: Boundary between diagnostics and neighboring sections

bullets.diagnosticsVsAnalysis: Diagnostics answers the question "why the result degraded". It decomposes the cause instead of only showing the final metric.
bullets.diagnosticsVsGuide: Analysis answers the question "what happened on the full history". The route returns there after the cause is localized and its weight inside the total picture must be assessed.
bullets.diagnosticsVsDeveloper: Guide explains terms and rules. Developer shows the internal system structure and report assembly.

hotspots.title: Weak zones and missed trades
hotspots.description: Hotspots with low coverage, frequent NoTrade, and opposite decisions. Shows where the strategy breaks down and loses days.

control.text: Segments (OOS / Train / Full / Recent) define which data is used for metrics. "Business" mode shows aggregated indicators, while "Tech" mode shows detailed confusion matrices and distributions for model analysis.

terms.title: Model stats terms
terms.subtitle: Detailed definitions of all columns used in the current table set.

header.title: Prediction aggregation
header.subtitle: This page shows how the base forecast turns into the final trading-day answer: what [[landing-micro-model|Micro]] changes, what the [[sl-model|SL model]] changes, and how that shows up in quality.

probs.title: Aggregated probabilities
probs.hint: Average probabilities by segment. This shows what the base answer does, what [[landing-micro-model|Micro]] adds, and what the final result looks like after risk correction.

metrics.title: Quality metrics
metrics.hint: Quality of the same layers on the same segments. This shows which layer actually improves the result and which one only shifts the class.

debug.title: Recent-day review
debug.hint: Recent dates one by one: the day fact, layer outputs, probabilities, and cases where [[landing-micro-model|Micro]] or the [[sl-model|SL model]] actually intervened.
debug.empty: There are no rows for the recent-day review. This usually means the last-days block was not built together with the aggregation.

probTable.layer:
Layer means one stage of forecast assembly inside [[landing-aggregation|aggregation]].

What it shows:
[[current-prediction-daily-layer|Day]] is the base daily answer without micro refinement and without risk correction. Day+[[landing-micro-model|Micro]] is the answer after the micro layer. Total is the final answer after the full assembly and the effect of the [[sl-model|SL model]].

How to read it:
these rows should be read top to bottom as the path of one and the same forecast through three assembly stages. If the final layer shifts probabilities a lot, the useful question is whether quality improved or the class simply changed.

Example:
if Day shows a clear tilt toward up, Day+Micro moves the answer toward sideways, and Total cuts the probability of up even more, the final class was shaped inside the assembly layers, not in the first model alone.
probTable.pUp: Average probability of Up class for selected layer and segment.
probTable.pFlat: Average probability of Flat class for selected layer and segment.
probTable.pDown: Average probability of Down class for selected layer and segment.
probTable.sum: Average sum P_up+P_flat+P_down. Normally close to 1; strong drift indicates normalization issues.

pills.confDay:
Conf Day is the average [[landing-model-confidence|model-confidence]] signal of the base daily layer.

What it shows:
this is not a probability sum and not a share of correct answers. The field shows how strongly the base Day layer expressed its answer on average inside the segment.

How to read it:
a higher Conf Day means the base layer more often saw a more pronounced scenario, but it does not guarantee better quality. The signal should be compared with the layer metrics below.

Example:
if one segment has a high Conf Day but quality barely improves, the layer is becoming more confident without necessarily becoming better.
pills.confMicro: Average confidence signal of micro layer used inside flat regime. Separate from class probabilities.
pills.slScoreDays:
SL-score days is the number of days in the segment that received a non-zero risk score from the [[sl-model|SL model]].

What it shows:
this number separates segments where the risk layer almost never participated from segments where it regularly entered the final assembly.

How to read it:
if the value is small, the influence of SL on the final answer should be read cautiously because the layer was rarely active. If the value is large, the final answer depends more on risk correction and not only on the base forecast.

Example:
a value of 18 / 240 means the [[sl-model|SL model]] actually entered the calculation on only 18 days out of 240.

metrics.accuracy: Share of correct predictions: Correct / N. Higher is better.
metrics.microF1: Micro-F1 for 3-class task. In current implementation it equals Accuracy, but kept as a separate metric.
metrics.logLoss:
LogLoss is the average penalty for putting high confidence on the wrong answer.

What it shows:
the metric looks not only at the final class but also at the probabilities assigned to the true class. A lower penalty means the layer either guesses more often or makes mistakes without excessive overconfidence.

How to read it:
unlike plain hit-rate, LogLoss is sensitive to bad probability calibration. Two layers can have a similar share of correct answers, but the one that makes more overconfident mistakes will have a worse LogLoss.

Example:
two layers can have the same number of correct classifications, but if one of them puts a very high probability on the wrong class, its LogLoss will be noticeably worse.
metrics.nCorrect: N is total records in segment, Correct is number of predictions matching true label.
metrics.validInvalidLogLoss: Records used for log-loss. Invalid > 0 means invalid probabilities (ideally 0).

confusion.truePred: Confusion matrix: rows are true labels, columns are predicted labels. Values are day counts.

labels.flat: FLAT class: sideways day without strong trend.

debug.dateUtc: Trading day date (UTC) for this row.
debug.true: True day class (fact): UP / FLAT / DOWN.
debug.day: Prediction of base day model (before micro/SL overlays).
debug.dayMicro: Prediction after micro overlay is applied.
debug.pDay: Probability triple Up/Flat/Down for base Day model.
debug.pDayMicro: Probability triple Up/Flat/Down after micro overlay.
debug.pTotal: Final Up/Flat/Down probabilities after SL overlay.
debug.microUsed:
Micro used is the flag that [[landing-micro-model|Micro]] really intervened in the intermediate answer between Day and Day+Micro.

What it shows:
`yes` means the micro layer did not just calculate its own probabilities, but actually changed the intermediate answer or its balance. `no` means the base Day layer passed further without that intervention.

How to read it:
this column is the quick way to separate days where the final answer changed because of [[landing-micro-model|Micro]] from days where the final result was inherited almost entirely from the base layer.

Example:
if the base and final answers differ but `Micro used = no`, the reason for the final shift does not sit in the micro layer but lower in the stack, for example in risk correction.
debug.slUsed: Flag that SL overlay affected final outcome (Day+Micro → Total).
debug.microAgree: Whether Day and Day+Micro labels match (true = unchanged).
debug.slPenLong: SL overlay reduced long probability (penalty to P_up).

page.title: Prediction history

intro.title: What prediction history shows
intro.lead: Prediction history is the archive of past forecast reports by date.
bullets: Each date keeps the original forecast and the realized outcome of the same [[landing-time-horizon|trading day]] after it closes.
This screen is used to check repeatability: where the strategy held, where it failed, and how the result changed from date to date.
[[landing-backtest-summary|Historical simulation]] shows the long distance as a whole, while prediction history shows separate days and the reports behind them.

trainDiagnosticsIntro.title: What train diagnostics shows
trainDiagnosticsIntro.lead: Train diagnostics is the mode for reviewing model mistakes inside the training slice.
bullets: This mode does not pretend to be user-facing prediction history. It surfaces error slices and weak spots on familiar history.
The section shows worst mistakes, confident mistakes, and borderline days with low decision margin.
Honest transfer to new days is still checked through OOS evaluation, while the freshest part of that evaluation lives in the recent OOS tail.

items.item1: Backtest is a simulation of how the strategy would trade on historical data.
items.item2: All Policy configurations are computed on the same period and identical source data, so Policy-to-Policy comparison is valid.
items.item3: Differences between Policy configurations reflect strategy-rule differences, not differences in market history.

items.policy: Policy: when to open a position, in which direction to open (LONG, SHORT, or skip day), what size to use, and where TP/SL are set.
items.branch:
Branch is an execution scenario for the same Policy.

BASE: uses original daily signal direction without inversion (LONG stays LONG, SHORT stays SHORT).

ANTI-D: anti-direction rule is checked daily. If triggered, direction is forcibly inverted (LONG -> SHORT, SHORT -> LONG).

One mandatory anti-direction filter: MinMove in working range (0.5%-12%).
items.slMode: SL Mode: WITH-SL enables stop-loss (exit priority liquidation -> stop-loss -> take-profit -> EndOfDay). NO-SL disables stop-loss (exit only by take-profit, liquidation, or EndOfDay); this can wipe bucket capital and, in cross margin, the entire trading balance.
items.tpSlMode: TP/SL mode: ALL shows all trades, DYNAMIC shows only trades with actually applied dynamic levels, STATIC shows only trades with fixed levels.
items.zonal: ZONAL: WITH applies zonal entry filter, WITHOUT runs without this filter.
items.metricView: METRIC VIEW: REAL shows factual result, NO BIGGEST LIQ LOSS removes one largest liquidation only for tail-risk analysis. This is counterfactual mode, not real final return.
items.bucket: bucket: daily/intraday/delayed are independent slices with separate capital; total is aggregate sum of their metrics.

reading.title: How to read / interpret the table

items.item1: Reading order: 1) emergency risk first (HadLiq and AccRuin), 2) then drawdown depth (MaxDD%), 3) then recovery difficulty (Recovered, RecovDays, ReqGain%), 4) only after that final return (TotalPnl% and Wealth%).
items.item2: Comparison is valid only inside one slice: same bucket, TP/SL mode, SL Mode, ZONAL, and Metric View.
items.item3: If NO BIGGEST LIQ LOSS is significantly better than REAL, strategy result critically depends on one extreme failure.

items.item1: 3.1 Position (trade): LONG is a bet on price growth. SHORT is a bet on price decline. If filters block the day, no position is opened (no-trade).
items.item2: 3.2 Leverage: multiplier over margin. 10x leverage means ~1% price move gives ~10% margin-level result.
items.item3: 3.3 Margin: funds allocated to position. Isolated limits risk to that position margin. Cross uses shared deposit as margin, and liquidation can wipe full balance.
items.item4: 3.4 Stop-Loss and Take-Profit: Stop-Loss auto-closes on loss, Take-Profit auto-locks profit.
items.item5: 3.5 Liquidation: exchange force-closes position when loss almost consumes margin. In Isolated, position margin is lost; in Cross, liquidation may wipe full deposit.
items.item6: 3.6 Backtest: strategy check on history. Useful for idea/risk comparison, but does not guarantee future behavior.

simulation.title: What simulation includes

items.item2: Funding: not included, so real exchange result may differ for long-held positions.
items.item4Prefix: Event order inside first-event rule: liquidation → stop-loss → take-profit →

simulation.endOfDayTooltip: EndOfDay (EOD) means time-based close at end of trading window if TP, SL, or liquidation did not trigger earlier.

part2Diagnostics.title: Detailed risk diagnostics
part2Diagnostics.description: Part 2/4 aggregates balance and recovery metrics by Policy/Branch. The risk page expands the same emergency scenarios into dedicated diagnostics tables and shows where losses are formed.

items.item1: [[liquidation|Liquidations]] and [[account-ruin|AccRuin]]: shows which policies reach forced close or lose the working capital base.
items.item2: [[drawdown|Drawdown]], [[recovery|recovery]] and the capital curve: shows how deep capital fell and whether it returned to the previous peak.
